title: Trusting God: God's Sovereignty and Human Freedom — Are They Compatible?
speaker: Elder Andrew Hoy
source_files: God’s Sovereignty and Human Freedom - Are they compatible_ - Trusting God - Elder Andrew Hoy.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Laying Out Three Views
subpoints: Chapter 4 of Trusting God; Elder Anderson taught chapter 3 last week
The question: if God decrees all things, how can humans make genuine decisions?
Three views: the Deist view (God created and stepped back), the Interventionist view (God intervenes as needed), the Determinist/Reformed view (God decrees all things)
The Deist view is clearly unscriptural; the Interventionist view is common but inconsistently Christian

outline.title: God's Sovereignty: The Determinist/Reformed View
subpoints: Westminster Confession 3:1: God 'freely and unchangeably ordained whatsoever comes to pass'
R.C. Sproul: this doctrine is not uniquely Reformed — it is affirmed by classical Jewish, Muslim, and Christian orthodoxy
If something existed outside God's sovereign rule, that thing would have authority over God — and would become God
Key verses the Interventionist cannot explain away: Ephesians 1:11 ('works all things according to the counsel of his will'), Matthew 10:29, Proverbs 21:1
Isaiah 10:5-6: God uses the Assyrians as the 'rod of his anger' — even brutal warfare is ordained

outline.title: God's Sovereignty Over Evil Human Actions
subpoints: Isaiah 10:5-6: God sends Assyria to do brutal warfare against his own people — and will then punish Assyria for it
Acts 2:22-23: Christ was 'delivered by the determined purpose and foreknowledge of God' — and yet those who crucified him are accountable for their 'lawless' act
The most evil act in history — the murder of God's Son — was ordained by God
The way to guard God's holiness is not by denying his sovereignty but by affirming that God is not culpable for evil even as he ordains it

outline.title: Human Freedom: What the Bible Affirms
subpoints: Westminster Confession 9: God endured the will of man with 'natural liberty that is neither forced nor by any absolute necessity of nature determined to good or evil'
Matthew 17:12: people 'did to him whatever they wished' — true decisions are made
Genesis 3:6: Eve 'saw... she took... she ate' — the Bible speaks throughout as though humans make real choices
Deuteronomy 30:15-16: choices are set before people as genuine — choose life or death
Matthew 12:35: humans bring forth good or evil out of the treasure of their hearts — decisions flow from nature

outline.title: Compatibilism: Holding Both Truths Together
subpoints: Scripture upholds both: God decrees all things, and humans make genuine decisions and are responsible for them
Bridges: 'God is able and does move upon the hearts and minds of people... without violating or coercing their wills'
We must not think of God causing humans to act the way a human forces another human — God is an entirely different category of being
Three qualifications: God is not the author of sin; God is infinitely beyond us in his ways; people make real choices
Application: when speaking to those who suffer, we can affirm both that God hates the evil done against them and that it was part of his sovereign plan for their good

summary:
In this Sunday school lesson — the second in a series on Jerry Bridges' *Trusting God* — Elder Andrew Hoy tackles one of theology's most challenging questions: are God's absolute sovereignty and genuine human freedom compatible? Drawing on chapter 4 of Bridges' book, he organizes the lesson around three views of how God interacts with the world, argues for the biblical (Determinist/Reformed) view, and shows how it can hold human freedom and divine sovereignty simultaneously.

The three views are: the Deist view (God created the world and left it to run on its own), the Interventionist view (God has all power and intervenes as necessary to accomplish his purposes, but does not decree all particulars), and the Determinist view (God decrees whatsoever comes to pass, including all particular events). Elder Hoy argues that the Interventionist view — common in evangelical circles — fails to accomplish what it sets out to accomplish. It cannot genuinely protect God's holiness, and it ends up questioning whether God is truly God at all. R.C. Sproul is cited: the idea that God decrees all things is not uniquely Reformed but is the position of any consistent theism.

The lesson then turns to the most difficult application of this doctrine: God's sovereignty over evil human actions. Isaiah 10 and Acts 2:22-23 are examined in detail. The crucifixion of Christ was both determined by God and the result of 'lawless hands' — both divine ordination and human culpability are asserted simultaneously without contradiction.

The second half of the lesson affirms that Scripture also genuinely upholds human freedom — not the libertarian free will of philosophical discussion, but a real ability to make decisions in accordance with one's nature. The session closes by naming this position 'compatibilism': God decrees all things and humans make genuine decisions, and while we may not be able to explain perfectly how this works, we affirm both because Scripture does. The practical application is that Christians can honestly say to someone who has suffered evil: God hates what was done to you, and it was not outside his sovereign plan for your good.
transcript:
## Opening Prayer

We opened with the word of prayer. Great. Father, we thank you that you have called this together as your people today. Thank you for this congregation, the joy-filled hearts of so many of the people here and their desire to learn and to know your ways. I pray that in this morning's Sunday school lesson, your Spirit would be upon us, that he would use this lesson to strengthen our faith and our understanding of your Word, that we might faithfully follow you in all of life. We pray this in Christ's precious name. And everyone said amen.

## Introduction: The Question

So I was a math and middle school math teacher for about ten years, for those of you who weren't aware of that. And one of the things I discovered in being a middle school math teacher was that I became very frustrated with elementary school math teachers — because elementary math teachers are notorious for lying to their students. They say things like: *You can never subtract a bigger number from a smaller number.* And there are also implied lies — like the idea that the equal sign is just a fancy way of saying "and the answer is." Another common one: multiplication is just repeated addition.

The point is that as a middle school math teacher, I found myself continually bumping up against things that students had been taught in elementary school that I had to reshape and reframe so they could adequately understand the math I was trying to teach them. And that can be very difficult for a student who for years has had a certain idea ingrained in their mind — and then for me to say: that's wrong. This is how you should really think about it.

And so this morning we are talking about God's sovereignty and human freedom. Today's lesson is chapter 4 of Jerry Bridges' book *Trusting God*, which is what we are studying in our book studies right now. Last week, Elder Anderson taught on chapter 3, in which Bridges lays out the big picture of God's sovereignty. You'll see some overlap today, but with a greater focus on: if God is utterly and completely sovereign over everything that ever happens, how does that leave any space for humans to make genuine, true decisions of their own?

So this will be broken down into four parts: we'll briefly look at God's sovereignty, then the idea of human freedom, then how they work together, and finally some implications and applications.

Sometimes the conversation is framed in terms of God's sovereignty and human *freedom*. Sometimes in terms of God's sovereignty and human *responsibility*. But here's what we're essentially talking about: is it true that God ordains all things that come to pass? And if so, can it also be true that humans truly make decisions for themselves?

## Three Views of God's Interaction with the World

I want to lay out three essential views on how people think of God interacting with the world.

**The Deist View.** They believe there is a creator God who created all things, but after creating, essentially stepped back and let creation — including all humanity — run its own course. God doesn't intervene; he just watches. Think of God as a watchmaker: he wound up the watch, set everything in place, and let it go. This view is completely unscriptural. If someone confessionally holds to this position, I don't think they're a Christian.

**The Interventionist View.** Also believes God created all things. This view also sees in Scripture that God has all power and authority. God can and does intervene in the created world in any way and at any time he wants to. But in this view, God only intervenes here and there, as needed. God largely created the world and let it go — but he does intervene as necessary to accomplish his purposes. These people read in Scripture that God's purposes will eventually come about — but they believe he does that not by being utterly sovereign over all things, but by intervening as necessary. Most of this view comes from a desire to protect God from being the author of evil, and to guard a certain view of human freedom. I don't think this view is actually biblical, and I don't think it accomplishes what it sets out to accomplish.

**The Determinist/Reformed View.** Also believes God created all things and has all power and authority. But also that God *decrees* whatsoever comes to pass — that anything that has ever taken place in all of the history of creation, even the smallest moving atom, was foreordained by God to come to pass. So everything happens by God's sovereign hand.

Now the word "determinist" has some bad baggage. There is naturalistic determinism, which is not what I mean here. What I mean here is what some call *soft determinism* — or better, *compatibilism* — which I'll explain later.

The Westminster Confession chapter 3, paragraph 1 reads: *God from all eternity did, by the most wise and holy counsel of his own will, freely and unchangeably ordain whatsoever comes to pass.*

I was tempted to call this the "Reformed view" — but R.C. Sproul helped me on this. He argues that God ordaining whatsoever comes to pass from all eternity is not uniquely Reformed theology. He says: *It is a doctrine expressed in classical Jewish orthodoxy, Muslim orthodoxy, and Christian orthodoxy with respect to the nature of God. All of this does is affirm theism — it affirms that God is sovereign.* So this isn't a niche Calvinist position. It is simply the affirmation that God is God.

I was discussing this with my boys at school. Why would denying God's absolute sovereignty undermine the entire notion of God being God? One of my eighth graders responded: well, if there were something outside of God's sovereign rule — something he did not exercise complete sovereign control over — that thing would somehow have authority over God. And if God is the one with ultimate power and authority, then that other thing would become God. The point: if you deny that God ordains all things, you end up devolving into monism — the idea that there is one closed system of reality, and even God is part of it. R.C. Sproul: *If in some sense God does not ordain everything that comes to pass, he is not really sovereign. If he is not sovereign, he cannot be God.*

## Sovereignty in Scripture

Let me briefly look at some verses on God's sovereignty through the lens of the Interventionist and Determinist readings.

**Proverbs 19:21**: *Many are the plans in the mind of a man, but it is the purpose of the Lord that will stand.* The Determinist reads this as: God's purpose stands in all circumstances. The Interventionist reads it as: ultimately God's final outcomes will prevail — but he's not necessarily in control of all the details along the way, just the final outcome.

**Psalm 33:10-11**: *The Lord brings the counsel of the nations to nothing; he frustrates the plans of the peoples. The counsel of the Lord stands forever, the plans of his heart to all generations.* The Interventionist latches onto *he frustrates* — so God is just occasionally intervening to prevent things from going wrong. The Determinist reads this as God sovereignly working in all things.

**Isaiah 14:27**: *For the Lord of hosts has purposed, and who will annul it?* The Interventionist reads *purpose* as God's ultimate plans — not every particular event. The Determinist reads it as God's decree over all particulars.

But there are verses the Interventionist simply cannot explain:

**Ephesians 1:11**: *In him also we have obtained an inheritance, being predestined according to the purpose of him who works *all things* according to the counsel of his will.* My notes for the Interventionist position on this verse just have a question mark. I don't know how they get around *all things*.

**Matthew 10:29**: *Are not two sparrows sold for a copper coin? And not one of them falls to the ground apart from your Father's will.* An argument from the lesser to the greater. If something so small and insignificant as a sparrow dying cannot happen apart from God's will, how much more the greater things of life?

**Proverbs 21:1**: *The king's heart is in the hand of the Lord, like rivers of water; he turns it wherever he wishes.* In that era, if anybody could claim anything close to autonomy, it would be a king with absolute authority. And yet here we have an unqualified statement that God turns the king's heart wherever he wishes.

## God's Sovereignty Over Evil Actions

Now we need to look at some verses that deal with God's sovereignty over *evil* human actions. This is really where the Interventionist gets uncomfortable.

**Isaiah 10:5-6**: *Woe to Assyria, the rod of my anger, and the staff in whose hand is my indignation. I will send him against an ungodly nation, and against the people of my wrath I will give him charge, to seize the spoil, to take the prey, and to tread them down like the mire of the streets.* God sends Assyria — a pagan, brutal people — to do horrific violence against his own people. This is the *rod* of God's *anger*. Then later he says he will punish Assyria for what they did. Those who were the rod of his anger will be held accountable for their lawless acts.

**Acts 2:22-23**: *Men of Israel, hear these words: Jesus of Nazareth, a man attested to you by God with mighty works and wonders and signs which God did through him in your midst, as you yourselves know — him, being delivered by the determined purpose and foreknowledge of God, you have taken by lawless hands, have crucified, and put to death.* God was sovereign over that. And the people who crucified Christ are still responsible — "lawless hands." The most evil act in history was planned by God. And we cannot get God off the hook for evil by saying he wasn't sovereign over it.

So what is the answer? How do we resolve this? Well, before we get to the resolution, we have to establish that the Bible also genuinely affirms human freedom.

## Human Freedom: What Scripture Affirms

The Westminster Confession — which I already quoted saying God ordains all things — also has an entire chapter on free will (chapter 9). It opens: *God has endured the will of man with that natural liberty that is neither forced nor by any absolute necessity of nature determined to good or evil.*

So does Scripture actually teach that humans make real decisions?

**Matthew 17:12**: *But I say to you that Elijah has come already, and they did not know him, but did to him whatever they wished.* People actually made decisions. They are responsible for their actions.

**Genesis 3:6**: *So when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree desirable to make one wise, she took of its fruit and ate.* We should not read this as though God was making Eve do it. The Bible throughout speaks as though humans make real, authentic choices.

**Deuteronomy 30:15-16**: *See, I have set before you today life and good, death and evil, in that I command you today to love the Lord your God, to walk in his ways, and to keep his commandments.* The Bible constantly gives commands and presents them to people as though they have a genuine choice.

**Matthew 12:35**: *A good man out of the good treasure of his heart brings forth good things, and an evil man out of the evil treasure brings forth evil things.* Humans make true decisions — decisions that are in accordance with their desires and their nature. A sinful, unregenerate person will never choose God. A believer who has a renewed nature has the ability to turn from sin into righteousness. So yes, humans make true decisions — decisions in accordance with their nature.

Bridges summarizes it this way: *God is able and does move upon the hearts and minds of people to accomplish his purposes. Yet it also seems equally clear from Scripture that God does this without violating or coercing their wills. Rather, he works in his mysterious way through their wills to accomplish his purposes.*

## Compatibilism: The Answer to the Question

So — are they compatible? God's sovereignty and human freedom? The answer is: *they have to be.* Because Scripture upholds them both.

Scripture shows that God supremely governs and decrees all things which come to pass. Scripture also says that humans make true decisions and are responsible for those decisions. Theologians call this position *compatibilism*.

Now if you want to ask: *how* do they work side by side? You don't have to know. You just have to believe what Scripture says.

Does that create some intellectual discomfort? For me, yes. God is in control, but we're responsible. We make real decisions, but God is decreeing it. That sounds confusing. Are we being anti-intellectual? I would say not. There are aspects of reality that are simply beyond human comprehension. We know this if we believe in a God who is holy and righteous and far beyond us.

The conversation around sovereignty and human freedom has to be hedged in by some important qualifications:

1. **God is infinitely beyond us in his ways and in his being.** When we say God decrees all things, we must not think of this the way we think of a human causing another human to act. If I grabbed Zain's arm and made him slap someone, he might not be responsible for that. But God causing someone to act is an entirely different category — God is not a human. So remember that God is a completely other category of being.

2. **God is not the author of sin.** There is no evil in God. God cannot sin. So there is a true sense in which God can decree sinful actions without being responsible for them or sinning himself. We must simply be consistent with what Scripture says.

3. **People make real choices.** We've already established this from Scripture.

If we remove any of these pieces from the discussion, we drift into dangerous territory.

## Application

This is an opportunity to testify to our belief in the authority and inerrancy of Scripture. I see Scripture teaches this. I see Scripture teaches that. I cannot perfectly reconcile them for you. But I know I believe in a holy, righteous, good, and faithful God, and that all of these things are true.

Also — when talking to people who have suffered, who have experienced true evil — we can faithfully say both things that Scripture says. We can say: *God hates that evil action that was done against you. It is wicked, and whoever the perpetrator is will one day face judgment, unless he finds redemption in Christ.* And we can also affirm: *God is in control of all things. This did not happen by divine oversight. It was part of God's sovereign plan, that God in eternity past decreed that it happened this way. And God is able to work all things according to the counsel of his will for your good.*

Let us pray.

Merciful God, we know that you are a holy, righteous, and good Father. We know that only you are perfectly holy and perfectly good. I pray that if nothing else, as we consider the truths of what your Word holds, you will have been lifted up high in our hearts and our minds this morning. You are absolutely in control, and your ways are beyond our ways. May we not forget that there will be a day where every evil act that has ever taken place will be taken to account and judged — either atoned for in the blood of your Son, or having the eternal consequence of hell as the just punishment. Father, we thank you that we can rest in the authority and truth of your Scripture. I pray that we do exactly that today, and that our hearts and minds will be prepared to worship you with your people this morning as well. We pray this all in the precious name of our Savior Jesus Christ. And everyone said amen.
